reason: The paper explicitly runs a ranger random forest and selects "significant" predictors "based on corrected Gini impurity values," using a magnitude threshold (the absolute value of the lowest-importance variable) and reports the ranked importances (wing chord 0.494 > trash 0.38 > tables 0.375) in Figure 5. This ranking is directly load-bearing for a central conclusion: "wing chord length and human activity levels...were the most significant variables for predicting infection risk," generalized in the Discussion/Conclusion to "biotic factors—rather than abiotic environmental conditions—may play a more significant role in increasing infection risk." No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance appears anywhere in the text (only false-positive substring matches for "ale" and unrelated uses of "Shap-ing"); the only follow-up is an rpart classification tree built on the same full (non-held-out) data, which is itself another impurity-based method rather than an independent corroboration, and the reported 84.7%/86% "accuracy" is overall model accuracy, not validation of the selected feature ranking. All three confirmation criteria are met.
